character.name: Wren Okafor-Silva

appearance.brief: Medium height, brown skin, tight coils she usually pulls into a messy top-knot with a pencil stuck through it because she 'forgot' hair ties again. Wears mismatched thrifted layers — a band tee under a cardigan from her abuela, chunky sneakers held together with paint pen doodles.
distinctiveFeatures: A chipped front tooth from a skateboarding fall she tells three different stories about
Draws tiny stars on the back of her hand in pen when she's nervous
Wears her late grandfather's watch, always five minutes fast

mannerisms: Mirrors the body language/slang of whoever she's talking to within minutes of meeting them
Cracks a joke the second a room goes quiet or tense
Chews the inside of her cheek when deciding whether to speak up
speechPatterns: Code-switches fluidly — formal with adults, clipped and sarcastic with peers she's testing out
Uses questions as a shield ('Wait, is that weird? No? Cool, cool.')
Drops one-liners fast, then goes quiet right after like she's checking if it landed
personality.emotionalTendencies: Wren reads a room in seconds and adjusts herself to fit it — she's been the 'new kid' four times before and has learned that fitting in fast means surviving. Underneath the quick jokes is a kid exhausted from constantly auditioning for belonging, terrified that if people saw the unedited version of her, they'd pass.

background.family: Only child of a Nigerian dad (traveling nurse, reason for the moves) and Brazilian-American mom (teaches ESL). Close with her mom, complicated with her dad — she loves him but resents that his job keeps uprooting her life. Her abuela lives two states away and is the one person Wren is never performing for on the phone.
interests: collecting stickers/pins from every city she's lived in
drawing tiny comics in the margins of her notebooks
strengths: genuinely funny — can defuse tension in a room
fiercely protective of people she decides are 'hers'
struggles: doesn't know who she is without an audience to calibrate against
avoids conflict by joking it away, even when she should speak up
low-grade dread that every friendship has an expiration date, so why get attached
secrets: She has a notebook with a running list titled 'Who I Was At Each School' — different names for her different personas (Skater Wren, Nice Wren, Funny Wren)
She cried in the car the morning of the move and told her dad she was 'just tired'

conscious: Find a table to sit at that doesn't feel like a test she can fail
Not be 'the weird new kid' by 3rd period
Make it to winter break without having to switch schools again
unconscious: Be liked for who she actually is, not the version she performs
Stop treating every new place like it requires a new self
Trust that people won't leave/be left before she does

voiceGuidelines.vocabularyLevel: Contemporary middle-school casual with some quick wit; references pop culture and podcasts, never sounds like an adult wrote her a lesson.
voiceGuidelines.sentenceComplexity: Short, punchy sentences when joking or deflecting; longer, run-on thoughts when she's anxious and overexplaining.
voiceGuidelines.emotionalExpressiveness: Expressive through humor and small physical tells (the star-doodles, cheek-chewing) rather than direct statements — she'll joke about something true before she'll say it plainly.
examples: "Oh, I'm not nervous. This is just my face. I have a nervous face and a normal face and they're identical, it's a whole medical thing."
"Cool, cool, cool — so is this a 'sit wherever' cafeteria or is this a 'sit wherever and regret it for the rest of the year' cafeteria?"
"I've done first days at four schools. I have a system. The system is: pretend you already know where you're going, even when you very much do not."
"...No, I don't need you to save me a seat. I mean — unless you were gonna. Then, yeah. Obviously. Save me a seat."

character.storyRole: Player-character protagonist of 'First Bell.' Her practiced first-day survival system collides with real choices — who to sit with, who to defend, who to become — forcing her to notice, maybe for the first time, that the performance has a cost.
arcPotential: Episode 'First Bell': Wren must choose a lunch table, decide whether to stand up for a student getting mocked, and confront whether her chameleon act is protecting her or erasing her.
Episode 'The List': Wren's persona-notebook gets found/shared, forcing a reckoning with who she's been performing versus who she wants to be.
Episode 'Staying': As the semester ends without a move for once, Wren must decide if she's willing to let people know her long enough to actually be known.

designNotes: Wren's core wound is rootlessness — four schools have taught her that identity is a survival tool, not a stable thing. Her humor and social fluency (surface charm) mask a genuine fear of being truly seen and rejected, which makes her a strong vehicle for the episode's belonging/authenticity themes: every lunch-table choice is really a choice about which self to offer up. She's built to surprise — the funniest kid in the room is also the one most afraid to be known. Her arc isn't 'learn to be yourself' in a neat bow; it's messier — learning that adapting isn't inherently fake, but doing it out of fear instead of choice has a cost.
representationNotes: Wren is biracial (Nigerian/Brazilian-American), reflecting a multicultural urban community without reducing her to a 'diversity checkbox' — her heritage shows up in specific, lived-in details (abuela, code-switching, family dynamic) rather than as a plot device. Her military-adjacent transient family life is a relatable stand-in for the very real experience of frequently-moving kids (military families, immigrant families, economically mobile families) without naming one specific cause, keeping her broadly relatable to transfer students of many backgrounds.
